author: John Chrysostom
book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

classification.cautionReason: No major mainstream heresy warning is attached by the preview classifier.

lineage: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.

ccel.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

title: Matthew XII. 33.

source.title: NPNF1-10. St. Chrysostom: Homilies on the Gospel of Saint Matthew

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “Either make the tree good, and his fruit good, or else make the tree corrupt, and his fruit corrupt; for the tree is known by his fruit.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Again in another way He shames them, and is not content with His former refutations.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But this He doth, not freeing Himself from accusations, (for what went before was quite enough), but as wishing to amend them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now His meaning is like this: none of you hath either found fault about the persons healed, as not being healed; nor hath said, that it is an evil thing to deliver one from a devil.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For though they had been ever so shameless, they could not have said this.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since therefore they brought no charge against the works, but were defaming the Doer of them, He signifies that this accusation is against both the common modes of reasoning, and the congruity of the circumstances.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: A thing of aggravated shamelessness, not only to interpret maliciously, but also to make up such charges as are contrary to men’s common notions.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And see how free He is from contentiousness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He said not, “Make the tree good, forasmuch as the fruit also is good;” but, most entirely stopping their mouths, and exhibiting His own considerateness, and their insolence, He saith, Even if ye are minded to find fault with my works, I forbid it not at all, only bring not inconsistent and contradictory charges.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For thus were they sure to be most clearly detected, persisting against what was too palpable.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore to no purpose is your maliciousness, saith He, and your self-contradictory statements.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Because in truth the distinction of the tree is shown by the fruit, not the fruit by the tree; but ye do the contrary.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what if the tree be the origin of the fruit; yet it is the fruit that makes the tree to be known.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And it were consistent, either in blaming us to find fault with our works too, or praising these, to set us who do them free from these charges.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But now ye do the contrary; for having no fault to find with the works, which is the fruit, ye pass the opposite judgment upon the tree, calling me a demoniac; which is utter insanity.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, and what He had said before,this He establishes now also; that a good tree cannot bring forth evil fruit, nor again can the converse be.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that their charges were against all consistency and nature.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then since He is arguing not for Himself, but for the Spirit, He hath dealt out His reproof even as a torrent, saying, “O generation of vipers, how can ye, being evil, speak good things?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Now this is at once to accuse, and to give demonstration of His own sayings from their case.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For behold, saith He, ye being evil trees, cannot bring forth good fruit.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: I do not then marvel at your talking thus: for ye were both ill nurtured, being of wicked ancestors, and ye have acquired a bad mind.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And see how carefully, and without any hold for exception, He hath expressed His accusations: in that He said not, “How can ye speak good things, being a generation of vipers? (for this latter is nothing to the former): but, “How can ye, being evil, speak good things?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But He called them “broods of vipers,” because they prided themselves on their forefathers.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: To signify therefore that they had no advantage thereby, He both casts them out from their relationship to Abraham, and assigns them forefathers of kindred disposition, having stripped them of that ground of illustriousness.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For out of the abundance of the heart the mouth speaketh.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Here again He indicates His Godhead, which knew their secrets: and that not for words only, but also for wicked thoughts, they shall suffer punishment; and that He knows it all, as God.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He saith, that it is possible even for men to know these things; for this is a natural consequence, that when wickedness is overflowing within, its words should be poured forth through the lips.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that when thou hearest a man speak wicked words, do not suppose only so much wickedness to be in him as the words display, but conjecture the fountain to be much more abundant; for that which is spoken outwardly, is the superabundance of that which is within.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: See how vehemently He reprehends them.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if what they had said is so evil, and is of the very mind of the devil, consider the root and well-spring of their words, how far that must reach.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this is naturally the case; for while the tongue through shame often pours not forth all its wickedness at once, the heart having no human witness, fearlessly gives birth to whatever evils it will; for of God it hath not much regard.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Since then men’s sayings come to examination and are set before all, but the heart is concealed; therefore the evils of the former grow less, while those of the latter increase.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But when that within is multiplied, all that hath been awhile hidden comes forth with a violent gushing.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And as persons vomiting strive at first to keep down the humors that force their way out, but, when they are overcome, cast forth much abomination; so do they that devise evil things, and speak ill of their neighbors.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “A good man out of his good treasure,” saith He, “bringeth forth good things, and an evil man out of his evil treasure bringeth forth evil things.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For think not by any means, saith He, that it is so in respect of wickedness only, for in goodness also the same occurs: for there too the virtue within is more than the words without.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: By which He signified, that both they were to be accounted more wicked than their words indicated, and Himself more perfectly good than His sayings declared.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He calls it “a treasure,” indicating its abundance.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then again He fences them in with great terror.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For think not at all, saith He, that the thing stops at this, that is, at the condemnation of the multitude; nay, for all that do wickedly in such things shall suffer the utmost punishment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And He said not, “ye,” partly in order to instruct our whole race, partly to make His saying the less burdensome.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “But I say unto you,” this is His word, “that every idle word that men shall speak, they shall give account thereof in the day of judgment.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And that is idle, which is not according to the fact, which is false, which hath in it unjust accusation; and some say, that which is vain also, for instance, provoking inordinate laughter, or what is filthy, and immodest, and coarse.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: “For by thy words thou shalt be justified, and by thy words thou shalt be condemned.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou how far the tribunal is from invidiousness? how favorable the account required?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not upon what another hath said of thee, but from what thou hast thyself spoken, will the Judge give His sentence; which is of all things the very fairest: since surely with thee it rests, either to speak, or not to speak.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wherefore not those that are slandered, but the slanderers, have need to be anxious and to tremble.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the former are not constrained to answer for themselves touching the evil things which are said of them, but the latter will, for the evil they have spoken; and over these impends the whole danger.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So that the persons censured should be without anxiety, not being to give account of the evil that others have said; but the censurers have cause to be in anxiety, and to tremble, as being themselves to be dragged before the judgment-seat in that behalf.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this is indeed a diabolical snare, and a sin having in it no pleasure, but harm only.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Yea, and such an one is laying up an evil treasure in his soul.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if he that hath an evil humor in him doth himself first reap the fruits of the malady, much more he that is treasuring up in himself what is more bitter than any bile, I mean, wickedness, will suffer the utmost evils, gathering unto himself a grievous disease.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And it is evident from the things that He vomits out.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if they pain others so much, far more the soul that gives them birth.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus the plotter destroys himself first; just as he that treadson fire burns up himself, and he that smites adamant spites himself, and he that kicks against the pricks draws blood from himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For somewhat of this kind is he that knows how to suffer wrong, and to bear it manfully; he is adamant, and the pricks, and fire; but he that hath used himself to do wrong is feebler than any clay.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Not therefore to suffer wrong is evil, but to do it, and not to know how to bear being wronged.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For instance, how great wrongs did David endure!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: How great wrongsdid Saul commit!

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Which then was the stronger and happier? which the more wretched and miserable? was it not he that did wrong?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And mark it.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Saul had promised, if David should slay the Philistine, to take him for his son-in law, and to give him his daughter with great favor.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: He slew the Philistine; the other broke his engagements, and so far from bestowing her, did even go about to slay him.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Which then became the more glorious?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Was not the one choking with despair and the evil demon, while the other shone brighter than the sun with his trophies, and his loyalty to God?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Again, before the choir of the women, was not the one suffocated with envy, while the other enduring all in silence, won all men, and bound them unto himself?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And when he had even gotten him into his hands, and spared him, which again was happy? and which wretched? which was the weaker? which the more powerful?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Was it not this man, who did not avenge himself even justly?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And very naturally.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For the one had armed soldiers, but the other, righteousness, that is more mighty than ten thousand armies, for his ally and helper.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And for this reason, though unjustly conspired against, he endured not to slay him even justly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he knew by what had taken place before, that not to do evil, but to suffer evil, this is what makes men more powerful.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So it is with bodies also, so also with trees.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what did Jacob?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Was he not injured by Laban, and suffered evil?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Which then was the stronger? he that had gotten the other into his hands, and durst not touch him, but was afraid and trembling;or he whom we see without arms and soldiers proving more terrible to him than innumerable kings?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But that I may give you another demonstration of what I have said, greater than this, let us again in the instance of David himself try the reasoning on the opposite side.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For this man who being injured was so strong, afterwards upon committing an injury became on the contrary the weaker party.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: At least, when he had wronged Uriah, his position was changed again, and the weakness passed to the wrong doer, and the might to the injured; for he being dead laid waste the other’s house.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And the one being a king, and alive, could do nothing, but the other, being but a soldier, and slain, turned upside down all that pertained to his adversary.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Would ye that in another way also I should make what I say plainer?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us look into their case, who avenge themselves even justly.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as to the wrong doers, that they are the most worthless of all men, warring against their own soul; this is surely plain to every one.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But who avenged himself justly, yet kindled innumerable ills, and pierced himself through with many calamities and sorrows?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: The captain of David’s host.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he both stirred up a grievous war, and suffered unnumbered evils; not one whereof would have happened, had he but known how to command himself.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Let us flee therefore from this sin, and neither in words nor deeds do our neighbors wrong.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For He said not, If thou slander, and summon a court of justice, but simply, If thou speak evil, though within thyself, even so shalt thou suffer the utmost punishment.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Though it be true which thou hast said, though thou have spoken upon conviction, even so shall vengeance come upon thee.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For not according to what the other hath done, but according to what thou hast spoken, will God pass sentence; “for by thy words thou shalt be condemned,” saith He.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Art thou not told that the Pharisee also spake the truth, and affirmed what was manifest to all men, without discovering what was hidden?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nevertheless, he paid the utmost penalty.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But if we ought not to accuse men of things which are acknowledged, much less of those which are disputed; nay, for the offender hath a judge.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Do not now, I warn thee, seize upon the privilege of the Only Begotten.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For Him is the throne of judgment reserved.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Wouldest thou however be a judge?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thou hast a court of judgment which hath great profit, and bears no blame.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Make consideration, as judge, to sit down upon thy conscience, and bring before it all thy transgressions, search out the sins of thy soul, and exact with strictness the account thereof, and say, “wherefore didst thou dare to do this and that?”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if she shun these, and be searching into other men’s matters, say to her, “Not about these am I judging thee, not for these art thou come here to plead.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what, if such a one be a wicked man?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thou, why didst thou commit this and that offense?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Answer for thyself, not to accuse; look to thine own matters, do not those of others.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And be thou continually urging her to this anxious trial.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Then, if she have nothing to say, but shrink back, wear her out with the scourge, like some restless and unchaste handmaid.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And this tribunal do thou cause to sit every day, and picture the river of fire, the venomous worm, the rest of the torments.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And permit her not to be with the devil any more, nor bear with her shameless sayings, “he comes to me, he plots against me, he tempts me;” but tell her, “If thou wert not willing, all that would be to no purpose.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if she say again, “I am entangled with a body, I am clothed with flesh, I dwell in the world, I abide on earth;” tell her, “All these are excuses and pretexts.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For such an one too was encompassed with flesh, and such another dwelling in the world, and abiding on earth, is approved; and thou thyself too, when thou doest well, doest it encompassed with flesh.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if she be pained at hearing this, take not off thine hand; for she will not die, if thou smite her, but thou wilt save her from death.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if she say again, “Such an one provoked me,” tell her, “But it is in thy power not to be provoked; often at least thou hast restrained thine anger.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if she say, “The beauty of such a woman moved me;” tell her, “Yet wast thou able to have mastered thyself.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Bring forward those that have got the better, bring forward the first woman, who said, “The serpent beguiled me,”and yet was not acquitted of the blame.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And when thou art searching out these things, let no man be present, let no man disturb thee; but as the judges sit under curtains to judge, so do thou too, instead of curtains, seek a time and place of quiet.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And when after thy supper thou art risen up, and art about to lie down, then hold this thy judgment; this is the time convenient for thee, and the place, thy bed, and thy chamber.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: This the prophet likewise commanded, saying, “For the things which ye say in your hearts, be ye moved to compunction upon your beds.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And for small offenses require great satisfaction, that unto the great thou mayest never even approach.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If thou do this every day, thou wilt with confidence stand at that fearful judgment-seat.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: In this way Paul became clean; therefore also he said, “For if we judged ourselves, we should not be judged.”

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Thus did Job cleanse his sons.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For he that offered sacrifices for secret sins, much more did he require an account of such as were manifest.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But we do not so, but altogether the contrary.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For as soon as we are laid down to rest, we rather think over all our worldly matters; and some introduce unclean thoughts, some usuries, and contracts, and temporal cares.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And if we have a daughter, a virgin, we watch her strictly; but that which is more precious to us than a daughter, our soul, her we suffer to play the harlot and defile herself, introducing to her innumerable wicked thoughts.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And whether it be the love of covetousness, or that of luxury, or that of fair persons, or that of wrath, or be it what you will else that is minded to come in, we throw open the doors, and attract and invite it, and help it to defile our soul at its leisure.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And what can be more barbarous than this, to overlook our soul that is more precious than all, abused by so many adulterers, and so long companying with them, even until they are sated? which will never be.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: So it is, therefore, that when sleep overtakes us, then only do they depart from her; or rather not even then, for our dreams and imaginations furnish her with the same images.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Whence also, when day is come, the soul stored with such images often falls away to the actual performance of those fancies.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And thou, while into the apple of thine eye thou sufferest not so much as a grain of dust to enter, dost thou pass unnoticed thy soul, gathering to itself a heap of so great evils?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: When shall we then be able to clear out this filth, which we are daily laying up within us? when to cut up the thorns? when to sow the seed?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Knowest thou not that henceforth the time of harvest is at hand?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But we have not yet so much as ploughed our fields.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If then the husbandman should come and find fault, what shall we say? and what answer shall we make?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That no man gave us the seed?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, this is sown daily.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: That no man, then, hath cut up the thorns?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, every day we are sharpening the sickle.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: But do the necessary engagements of life distract thee?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: And why hast thou not crucified thyself to the world?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For if he that repays that only, which is given him, is wicked, because he did not double it; he that hath wasted even this, what will be said to him?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If that person was bound, and cast out where is gnashing of teeth, what shall we have to suffer, who, when numberless motives are drawing us toward virtue, shrink back and are unwilling?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what is there, that hath not enough in it to persuade thee?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Seest thou not the vileness of the world, the uncertainty of life, the toil, the sweat, for things present?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What? is it the case that virtue must be toiled for, but may vice be had without toil?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: If then both in the one and in the other there is toil, why didst thou not choose this, which hath so great profit?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Or rather, there are some parts of virtue, which are free even from toil.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For what kind of toil is it, not to calumniate, not to lie, not to swear, to lay aside our anger against our neighbor?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: Nay, on the contrary, to do these things is toilsome, and brings much anxiety.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: What plea then shall we have, what excuse, not doing right even in these matters?

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: For hereby it is plain, that out of remissness and sloth the more toilsome duties also altogether escape us.

verses.book: The Homilies of St. John Chrysostom.
verses.text: All these things let us consider; let us flee vice, let us choose virtue, that we may attain both unto the good things that are present, and unto those that are to come, by the grace and love towards man of our Lord Jesus Christ, to whom be glory and might forever and ever.
